coredata.dc:title: Bright red-emitting P, Br co-doped carbon dots as “OFF-ON” fluorescent probe for Cu2+ and L-cysteine detection
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Weiying
Mo, Fuwang
Sun, Zishan
Luo, Jiabao
Fan, Jiaqi
Zhu, Hongni
Zhu, Zhenpeng
Huang, Jinqing
Zhang, Xinguo
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) with long-wavelength-emission and high quantum yield (QY) are the focus of current research, compared with widely-investigated blue-emitting CDs. Herein, bright red-emitting CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal reaction at 200 °C for 3 h. CDs prepared by p-Phenylenediamine (pPD) exhibits a weak red band at 635 nm under an excitation wavelength of 450 nm. Adding (NH4)2HPO4 and KBr as P and Br precursors, the red emission peak at 610 nm, which shows a peak blue-shift about 25 nm with a dramatic emission enhancement up to 20-folds by co-doping of P, Br atoms into CDs. The corresponding QY value of P, Br-doped CDs (P, Br-CDs) was greatly improved to 11.3% as compared to bare CDs’ (0.55%). Furthermore, P, Br-CDs show a remarkable luminescent turn-off response after adding copper ions (Cu2+). In addition, the quenched luminescence of P, Br-CDs/Cu2+ complex could be recovered by the chelation between Cu2+ and L-cysteine (L-Cys) after adding L-Cys. This fluorescence “ON-OFF-ON” protocol could be applied for sequent Cu2+ and L-Cys detection, and offer a linear range of 0–150 μM and 0–100 μM with a limit of detection of 4.408 µM and 2.373 µM for Cu2+ and L-Cys, respectively. All results indicate that P, Br-CDs are of great potential for further biological and environmental application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescent probe
Copper ions
L-cysteine
OFF-ON

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Procedure for determination of Cu2+ and L-Cys
Fluorescence “ON-OFF” sensing of P, Br-CDs by Cu2+
Fluorescence “ON-OFF” sensing mechanism of P, Br-CDs by Cu2+
Fluorescence “OFF-ON” sensing for L-Cys and its mechanism

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The research was funded by Guangzhou Scientific Planning Program (No. 201804010260 ), National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21601081 ), Guangxi Natural Science Foundation (No. 2018GXNSFBA281209 ) and Open project of College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering , Qingdao University of Science and Technology ( QUSTHX201925 ). Jinqing Huang acknowledges the financial support from Science, Technology and Innovation Commission of Shenzhen Municipality (No. JCYJ20180306174930894 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The wide scan XPS spectrum of P, Br-CDs (A), ATR-FTIR spectra of P, Br-CDs (B). The TEM image of P, Br-CDs (C) and the higher magnification image of CDs (D). High resolution XPS of C 1s (E), N 1s (F), P 2p3 (G), O 1s (H), Br 3d5 (I) and K 2p3 (J) spectra of P, Br-CDs.

Comparison of CDs prepared at different molar ratios between pPD, DAP and KBr (λex=450 nm) (A). The fluorescence intensities of CDs prepared by different pPD, DAP and KBr ratio (B). The fluorescence intensities at different hydrothermal temperatures (160–220 °C) (C) for different reaction times (3–9 h) (D). The lifetime value and exponential fit of pPD-CDs and P, Br-CDs (F).

Excitation and emission spectra of P, Br-CDs (A). PL spectra of P, Br-CDs excited at different wavelengths (B) and the PL spectra of P, Br-CDs in various pH condition (C), different ionic strength (D) under 450 nm excitation. Relative fluorescence intensities in different temperature (E), Fluorescence intensities of P, Br-CDs in different concentrations (F).

Fluorescence intensity of P, Br-CDs in the presence of different 200 μM ions (A). After mixing other metal ions, the emission intensity of P, Br-CDs/Cu2+ (B). Fluorescence responses of P, Br-CDs in the presence of different concentrations of Cu2+ (λex =450 nm, λem =610 nm) (C), the relationship between the emission integrated area and the concentration of Cu2+ (D).
The lifetime value (A), the UV–Vis absorption spectra (B), zeta potential (C) and the size (D) of P, Br-CDs adding different concentration of Cu2+.

Fluorescence recovery results for quenching by Cu2+ with the addition of L-cys. Ⅰ, Ⅱ, Ⅲ and Ⅳ indicate P, Br-CDs, P, Br-CDs with 150 μM, 400 μL Cu2+, P, Br-CDs containing 150 μM, 400 μL Cu2+ after addition of 300 μM, 400 μL L-cys, and P, Br-CDs with 300 μM, 400 μL L-cys, respectively (A). Fluorescence responses of P, Br-CDs/Cu2+ after adding different concentrations of L-Cys (λex =450 nm, λem =610 nm) (B), the relationship between the emission integrated area and the concentration of L-Cys (C). The zeta potential (D) and the size (F) of P, Br-CDs/Cu2+ complex in the presence of different concentration of L-Cys. The Scheme of Cu2+ and L-Cys “ON-OFF-ON” sensing based on P, Br-CDs (F).
Application of the proposed method for determination of samples of Cu2+ and L-Cys, the found was the value of three parallel experiments.

head.ce:title: Bright red-emitting P, Br co-doped carbon dots as “OFF-ON” fluorescent probe for Cu2+ and L-cysteine detection

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of New Drug Screening, School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southern Medical University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) with long-wavelength-emission and high quantum yield (QY) are the focus of current research, compared with widely-investigated blue-emitting CDs. Herein, bright red-emitting CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal reaction at 200 °C for 3 h. CDs prepared by p-Phenylenediamine (pPD) exhibits a weak red band at 635 nm under an excitation wavelength of 450 nm. Adding (NH4)2HPO4 and KBr as P and Br precursors, the red emission peak at 610 nm, which shows a peak blue-shift about 25 nm with a dramatic emission enhancement up to 20-folds by co-doping of P, Br atoms into CDs. The corresponding QY value of P, Br-doped CDs (P, Br-CDs) was greatly improved to 11.3% as compared to bare CDs’ (0.55%). Furthermore, P, Br-CDs show a remarkable luminescent turn-off response after adding copper ions (Cu2+). In addition, the quenched luminescence of P, Br-CDs/Cu2+ complex could be recovered by the chelation between Cu2+ and L-cysteine (L-Cys) after adding L-Cys. This fluorescence “ON-OFF-ON” protocol could be applied for sequent Cu2+ and L-Cys detection, and offer a linear range of 0–150 μM and 0–100 μM with a limit of detection of 4.408 µM and 2.373 µM for Cu2+ and L-Cys, respectively. All results indicate that P, Br-CDs are of great potential for further biological and environmental application.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), a kind of carbon-based fluorescent nano-material with enormous surface functional groups, have attracted great research attention since it was discovered in 2004 [1]. Owing to its unique optical performance, low toxicity, facile surface functionalization and good biocompatibility [2,3], CDs have been widely developed in fluorescent probes [4], biological field [5], lighting and displaying [6], catalyst and sensing [7], etc. However, the emission color of CDs mostly locates in blue and green region, which could be easily absorbed by the biological tissue and disturbed by serious background interference. Therefore, long-wavelength emitting CDs, which have intense emission in the far-red and near-infrared region (>600 nm), are in great demand owing to their advantages such as minimum tissue absorption, deep tissue penetration, low interference from autofluorescence and minimal photodamage [8]. Thus, the exploration of efficient red-emitting CDs with a high quantum yield, good photophysical and low toxicity is a major challenge in further CDs development.

Heavy metal ions pollution has drawn public’s attention by its toxic effects on plants and animals even at low concentrations [9]. Copper ions (Cu2+), which are the third most abundant transition metal ions after iron and zinc in the human body, could display an essential role in biological reaction [10]. Moreover, long time exposure to high levels and inadequate absorption of Cu2+ can lead to developing neurodegenerative diseases [11–13]. Actually, the accurate sensing of Cu2+ is of great importance in many fields, such as agriculture, chemical and physiological areas [14,15]. Meanwhile, L-cysteine (L-Cys) is an essential biothiol-containing amino acid maintaining redox homeostasis in physiology [16–18]. Abnormal level of L-Cys gives rise to the threat of diseases including developmental liver injuries and Alzheimer's disease, etc. [19,20] Notably, L-Cys is a strong Cu2+ binder and can remove Cu2+ from CDs-Cu2+-CDs base pairs, which could effectively turn on the fluorescence of CDs and exhibit high sensitivity/selectivity for L-Cys. In view of the significance and broad focus on Cu2+ and L-Cys, it is in great demand to exploit a relatively rapid and convenient strategy for tracing their concentrations. To date, there are many reported methods to detect them including wearable biosensors [21], electrochemical sensors [22], atomic absorption spectrometry [23] and so on. Compared to the methods mentioned above, fluorescent methods are favorable due to their merits of low-cost, environment-friendly and high sensitivity/selectivity [24,25].

Herein, a hydrothermal synthetic approach was established for synthesis of phosphorus and bromine co-doped CDs (P, Br-CDs) using p-Phenylenediamine (pPD), disodium hydrogen phosphate (DAP) and KBr as precursors. CDs prepared by pPD (pPD-CDs) exhibit a weak red band at 635 nm under an excitation wavelength of 450 nm. Compared with pPD-CDs with a quantum yield (QY) of 0.55%, P, Br-CDs exhibit a dramatic emission enhancement by 20 times, and realize a QY of 11.3% with a 25 nm blue-shift. P, Br-CDs act as promising fluorescence “turn-off” probe for Cu2+ ion, exhibiting a linear range of 0–150 μM with a limit of detection of 4.408 µM. Furthermore, P, Br-CDs/Cu2+ fluorescence can be effectively recovered by virtue of a specific reaction of L-Cys with Cu2+. This fluorescence “turn-on” protocol was applied to determine L-Cys with a linear range of 0–100 μM and a detection limit of 2.373 µM.

Phenylenediamine (pPD), ammonium phosphatedi basic (DAP), KBr, NaCl, Pb2+, Al3+, Cd2+, As3+, Co2+, Hg2+, Fe2+, PO4 3-, Mn2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Zn2+(1000 mg/L) and L-Cys were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. PBS basic (pH=6.5) was bought from Shanghai yuanye Bio-Technology Co. Ltd. All reagents and chemicals were analytical and the water used in the experiments was deionized.

P, Br-CDs were prepared with 0.108 g pPD, 0.396 g DAP and 0.833 g KBr in 10 mL deionized water dispersed by sonicating for 10 min, and transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 3 h. For comparison, 0.108 g pPD was reacted under the same conditions to obtain pPD-CDs. Then, CDs solutions filtered through a 0.22 µm microporous membrane.

To evaluate the selectivity of Cu2+, different metal ions (Pb2+, Al3+, Cd2+, As3+, Co2+, Hg2+, Fe2+, PO4 3-, Mn2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, 200 μM) were also prepared. The determination of Cu2+ was performed by adding various concentrations of Cu2+ solutions (0–150 μM, 400 μL, deionized water as solvent) to P, Br-CDs solution (135 mg/mL, PBS as solvent, pH 6.5) and mixing thoroughly. As for L-Cys, different concentrations of L-Cys (0–100 μM, 400 μL, deionized water as solvent) was added to 3 mL of CDs/Cu2+ solution. The PL was recorded at the excitation of 450 nm after 5 min for equilibration.

The QY of P, Br-CDs in water was measured, and Rhodamine B (RhB) (relative QY 56%, ethanol as solvent) was chosen as a standard reported [26]. The absorbance of all samples kept below 0.10 to minimize the re-absorption effects [27]. Then, the fluorescence emission was obtained at the excitation of 450 nm. The QY was calculated by using the equation:
Q =
Q
R
I
I
R
A
R
A
n
2
n
R
2
where “Q” is the QY, “I” is the integrated emission intensity, “n” is the refractive index, and “A” represents the optical density. “R” represents to the standard (Rhodamine B) of known QY (because of the low concentration of samples, the effect of solute could be ignored).

XPS was measured by X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (ESCALAB 250Xi). FT-IR was recorded using Bruker/TENSOR Ⅱ. TEM was performed by Tecnai F20 field emission microscope. UV–Vis absorption spectrum was collected by Metash UV-5200 spectrophotometer. DLS and Zeta potential measurement were performed by a Zetasizer Nano-ZS (Malvern Instruments). Luminescent spectra and fluorescence lifetime were obtained by Edinburgh FLS980 spectrometer. Quantum yield data was obtained by the method of Ref. [28].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for determination of Cu2+ and L-Cys
ce:section.ce:para: The determinations of Cu2+ and L-Cys were performed in the same conditions as the above. Briefly, P, Br-CDs (135 mg/mL, 150 μL), PBS (2050 μL), and various concentrations of 800 μL Cu2+ solutions were added to a cuvette. For L-Cys, P, Br-CDs (135 mg/mL, 150 μL), PBS (2050 μL), Cu2+ (150 µM) and various concentrations of 800 μL L-Cys solutions were added to a cuvette. For analysis of real samples, various amounts of Cu2+ and L-Cys were spiked to the samples of Cu2+ and L-Cys (25 µM). Each test was repeated three times and then calculated the corresponding recovery and relative standard deviation (RSD).

XPS, FT-IR, TEM are used to analyze the characterization of P, Br-CDs. The full survey XPS spectrum ( Fig. 1A) shows binding energy peaks at 63.1, 131.1, 283.5, 304.0, 415.6 and 532.5 eV for Br (3d5), P (2p3), C (1s), K (2p3), N (1s) and O (1s), respectively. As seen in Fig. 1E, four subpeaks from the C (1s) peaks are at 293.8 eV (C-Br), 287.6 eV (CC-N), 285.6 eV (C-O), and 283.0 eV (CC/C-C)[29]. In Fig. 1F, the N (1s) spectrum has two peaks, which are assigned to N-(H)4 at 401.8 eV and N-(O)3 at 409.2 eV. The P (2p3) spectrum (Fig. 1G) shows two peaks, which are assigned to P-C/P-N at 134.8 eV and P-O at 136.4 eV [30]. In addition, there are two subpeaks at 532.8 eV and 534.2 eV in the O (1 s) spectrum (Fig. 1H), which are attributed to C-OH/C-O-C and N-O, respectively. As for the Br (3d5) spectrum (Fig. 1I), the peaks at 66.4 eV and 71.0 eV are attributed to Br 3d5 and Br 3d3. While the K (2p3) spectrum (Fig. 1J) shows that the peak at 293.6 eV is assigned to K 2p3, and 305.5 eV can be attributed to K loss, which may come from the precursor.

The surface functional groups on P, Br-CDs were analyzed by ATR-FTIR spectra (Fig. 1B). The peaks at 3669 cm−1 was owing to -OH bond. N-H stretching vibrations and broad hydroxyl (-OH) peak are located at 3306 cm−1. The -CC–H bonds were formed at 2964 cm−1. The peak at 2893 cm−1 was attributed to the presence of -CH. The peak near 1374 cm−1 was assigned to C-N/CN stretching vibration, while the peaks centering at 1235 cm−1 stem from aliphatic C–O stretching. Also, PO stretching was observed at 1056 cm−1 [31], while the peak at 872 cm−1 was indicative of the C-Br bond. The surface functional groups, such as -OH, -NH2, PO, C-Br, on P, Br-CDs enable their good dispersion and stability in water and nitrogen, phosphorus, bromine are successfully doped into the CDs. To study the fine structures of P, Br-CDs, a high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) was used to characterize CDs. As shown in Fig. 1C, the prepared CDs are spherical and well-dispersed nano-particles. Fig. 1D shows the obvious lattice fringes with an interspatial distance of 0.42 nm. The visible lattice structure of the macroscopic points corresponds to (3,1,1) plane of PDF#79–1715.

The photo-luminescent properties of pPD-CDs and P, Br-CDs were characterized and shown in Fig. 2A. Two broad bands (380 and 520 nm) were seen in the UV–vis absorption spectra, which are assigned to the π–π*and n–π* transition. Excited-state energy could be trapped in the defect surface states, followed by emission through radiative transitions. At the optimal excitation wavelength (450 nm), pPD-CDs emit a weak red emission peaking at 635 nm and QY for pPD-CDs is calculated to be 0.55%, which indicates that prepared pPD-CDs are ideal red CDs except for their low QY. In general, the key to improve QY is to suppress non-radiative transitions and control the electronic transition process of surface states [31–33]. Considering the electron-withdrawing effect of heteroatoms, inhibiting non-radiative transitions with p-type doping is most likely to improve the QY [34,35]. Phosphorus doping can enhance the luminescence efficiency of CDs owing to the coexistence of defect sites and isolated sp2 carbon clusters, which could increase the band-gap into the UV–vis region and produce stronger fluorescence than individual sp2 carbon clusters. The fluorescence enhancement may also arise due to more isolated sp2 carbon clusters produced by increasing phosphorus content into CDs [32]. Furthermore, bromine acts as an electron acceptor because of a higher electronegativity when linked with the conjugation structure in CDs, making charge transfer obvious and producing high QY. The introduction of bromine can produce a new surface state that traps electrons, increases radiative recombination, and suppresses nonradiative recombination [33]. It is found in Fig. 2A that a great emission enhancement could be realized by P, Br co-doping. The corresponding PL intensity of optimal-composition P, Br-CDs is 21 times of pPD-CDs, along with the emission blue-shift of 25 nm. The QY of P, Br-CDs is 11.3% using RhB as reference, which is a concrete evidence that the P, Br co-doping is a convenient and efficient strategy to enhance PLQY of red CDs. Fig. 2B shows the QY varies with changing KBr and DAP concentration, which reaches its maximum at 11.3% at the molar ratio of 1:3:7. With further increasing dopant concentration, QY declines since excess dopant ions will form the quenching center in CDs, which captures excited energy and transforms it into non-radiative transition [34]. Moreover, the effects of hydrothermal temperatures (160–220 °C, Fig. 2C) and reaction times (3–9 h, Fig. 2D) on luminescence were investigated. The maximum fluorescence intensity was measured under the optimal experimental conditions (pPD: DAP: KBr = 1: 3: 7, 200 °C and 3 h). Furthermore, all decay curves can be explored and well fitted to a bi-exponential decay model (Fig. 2E):
I
( t )
=
I
0
+
A
1
exp ( −
t
τ
1
) +
A
2
exp ( −
t
τ
2
)
where, I is the luminescence intensity; τ
1 and τ
2 are the life components in bi-exponential decay model; A
1 and A
2 are constants; t is the time. And the effective decay lifetime values were calculated by:
τ
a v g
=
A
1
τ
1
2
+
A
2
τ
2
2
A
1
τ
1
+
A
2
τ
2

The average fluorescence lifetimes of pPD-CDs and P, Br-CDs are calculated as 2.721 and 2.033 ns, which are consistent with previous reported CDs decay lifetime [35]. The shorter lifetime for P, Br-CDs was ascribed to radiative recombination of carbon core excitations [36,37].

Optical properties of P, Br-CDs are shown in Fig. 3A. P, Br-CDs have a single red emission peak at 610 nm with an excitation peak at 450 nm. P, Br-CDs exhibit outstanding solubility in water and form a clear red solution at daylight, while the strong red light could be observed at blue excitation. In addition, P, Br-CDs exhibit excitation-dependent feature under different excitation wavelengths (Fig. 3B). When the excitation wavelength is increased from 350 to 510 nm, the emission intensity of P, Br-CDs rises to a maximum at 450 nm excitation and then decreases with increasing excitation wavelength; The maximum emission peak position is virtually excitation-dependent, with a gradually red-shift from 556 to 630 nm of increasing excitation wavelength. These phenomena are possibly ascribed to various luminescence centers or surface defect states[38]. We also investigated the emission consistency of P, Br-CDs under a variety of conditions such as pH values, ionic strength, temperatures and concentrations. In Fig. 3C, the fluorescence intensity is strongly influenced by pH value, which indicates P, Br-CDs are pH-dependent from 2 to 12. When the pH is below 7, surface functional groups are protonated. As the protonated surface functional groups have a much lower electron-donating efficiency, the fluorescence intensity of P, Br-CDs decreases. Strong red emission is maintained with pH value ranging from 8 to 12, indicating that P, Br-CDs stay good fluorescence at physiologic environment. As shown in Fig. 3D, the emission intensity remains constant even at high ionic strength up to 2.0 mol/L, which demonstrates P, Br-CDs have superior ionic capability. To know the thermal stability, the fluorescence intensity is studied from 15 to 60 °C. As depicted in Fig. 3E, it stays above 70% below 45 °C, and a thermal quench happens under higher temperature. Thus, P, Br-CDs are relatively stable at room temperature. The effect of the concentration of P, Br-CDs is also investigated by obtaining the fluorescence intensity from 45 mg/mL to 225 mg/mL. As shown in Fig. 3F, the optimal concentration is 135 mg/mL. These stability tests demonstrate that red-emitting P, Br-CDs have potential for practical application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence “ON-OFF” sensing of P, Br-CDs by Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para:
To explore the feasibility of P, Br-CDs for detection Cu2+, the series of experiments were conducted to prove its selectivity and sensitivity. Firstly, the fluorescence responses of P, Br-CDs solution (PBS as solvent, pH 6.5) were studied after adding metal ions solutions (Pb2+, Al3+, Cd2+, As3+, Co2+, Hg2+, Fe2+, PO4 3-, Mn2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, 200 μM) to the sensing system. As depicted in Fig. 4A, only Cu2+ could quench the fluorescence remarkably and have the highest quenching rate. I
0 and I represent the integrated emission intensities in the absence and presence of metal ions. Therefore, P, Br-CDs have the potential to detect Cu2+ in aqueous solution. What’s more, even after mixing other metal ions, the emission intensity of CDs/Cu2+ is still almost constant (Fig. 4B), which also suggesting its potential as a new fluorescence probe for Cu2+.

As shown in Fig. 4C, with increasing concentration of Cu2+ (0, 5, 10, 20, 50, 80, 100, 120, 150 μM), the fluorescence intensity decreases continuously. The relationship between the relative fluorescence intensity of P, Br-CDs and the concentration of Cu2+ could be described by the Stern-Volmer equation: I/I
0 = - 0.00372[c]+ 0.9954, where I
0 and I are the integrated emission intensities at 610 nm in the absence and presence of Cu2+, respectively. The resulting plot, as shown in Fig. 4D, exhibits a good linear relationship ranging from 0 μM to 150 μM (R
2 = 0.995). The limit of detection (LOD) is s calculated to be 4.408 µM by LOD = 3σ/S, where σ represents standard deviation of the low concentration sample and S is the slope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence “ON-OFF” sensing mechanism of P, Br-CDs by Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: In general, fluorescence quenching mechanisms of CDs towards metal ions can be concluded into several categories, including static quenching, dynamic quenching, Förster resonance energy transfer, photo-induced electron transfer, surface energy transfer, Dexter energy transfer and inner filter effect [39]. To figure out the quenching mechanism, a series of experiments were carried out. The lifetimes of P, Br-CDs with different concentration of Cu2+ were measured. Fig. 5A shows the comparison of decay lifetimes between P, Br-CDs and P, Br-CDs/Cu2+, in which almost no change is observed and reveals that static quenching is the corresponding mechanism of fluorescence quenching. The UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 5B) shows that as the concentration of Cu2+ increasing, the absorption intensity increases and the position of the peak is unchanged. This result proves the formation of CDs/Cu2+ complex leads to great variations in the optical properties and electronic structure of P, Br-CDs [40]. The absorption spectrum of CDs/Cu2+ complex overlaps with that of P, Br-CDs. The competitive absorption between CDs/Cu2+ complex and CDs eventually weakens the absorption efficiency of CDs and then quenches the fluorescence.

To further probe the quenching mechanism, both zeta potential and size distribution are applied. According to Fig. 5C, P, Br-CDs is negatively charged, with a ζ potential of −4.7 mV attributed to the carboxyl and hydroxyl groups at their surfaces, indicating great dispensability and stability in the PBS solution, which can form salt bridges to neutralize the ζ potential [41]. However, the ζ potential of P, Br-CDs after the addition of Cu2+ turns to positive and rises continuously with the augmentation of the concentration of Cu2+. Fig. 5C suggests the electrostatic interact of Cu2+ and oxygen-based functional groups increases the charge of P, Br-CDs. The variation of the size of CDs/Cu2+ complex can be also attributed to the bonding of the negative functional groups on P, Br-CDs surface and Cu2+ [42]. As shown in Fig. 5D, the hydrodynamic size varies from 423.3 to 1288.3 nm after the addition of increasing concentration of Cu2+, indicating the formation of CDs/Cu2+ complex. Noticeably, DLS gives a hydrodynamic size of large aggregates in aqueous solution, whereas TEM provides a smaller size of CDs in a dried state.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence “OFF-ON” sensing for L-Cys and its mechanism
ce:section.ce:para:
It has been reported that L-Cys would chelate with Cu2+ by the thiol group [43]. Therefore, CDs/Cu2+ complex can be developed to detect L-Cys based on the competitive chelation among L-Cys, Cu2+ and CDs. Upon enough addition of L-Cys, the fluorescence response of P, Br-CDs, after quenched by Cu2+ (135 mg/mL P, Br-CDs and 150 μM Cu2+), could effectively return to 84% of its original emission intensity ( Fig. 6A). The rate did not return to 100%, which might be attributed to the weak interaction between P, Br-CDs and Cu2+ still in the system. As shown in Fig. 6B, the fluorescence intensity of P, Br-CDs/Cu2+ increases gradually with L-Cys concentration increasing (0, 4, 10, 20, 30, 50, 80, 100 μM). The relationship between the relative fluorescence intensity of P, Br-CDs/Cu2+ and L-Cys concentration could be described by the Stern-Volmer equation: I/I
0 = 0.00691[c]+ 1.0102. I
0 and I represent the integrated emission intensities at 610 nm in the presence and absence of L-Cys, respectively. The resulting plot, as shown in Fig. 6C, exhibits a good linear relationship from 0 μM to 100 μM (R
2 = 0.983). LOD is estimated to be 2.373 µM.

The “OFF-ON” mechanism is presented as follows. Without L-Cys, Cu2+ would chelate with carboxyl/hydroxyl groups on P, Br-CDs surface. However, the chelated bonds between thiol groups on L-Cys surface and metal ions are much stronger than that between P, Br-CDs and Cu2+ [44,45]. Thus, the formation of Cu2+/L-Cys chelates results in the collapse of CDs/Cu2+complex, followed by the recovery of P, Br-CDs’ red emission.

Similarly, to prove “OFF-ON” mechanism further, both zeta potential and size distribution were applied again. Different concentration of L-Cys (0–150 μM) was added to P, Br-CDs/Cu2+ solution (the concentration of Cu2+ is 150 µM). According to Fig. 6D, P, Br-CDs/Cu2+ complex with L-Cys is positively charged. The ζ potential changes from 17.4 to 3.5 mV attributed to the augmentation of the concentration of L-Cys, which may be the result of exposure of the carboxyl and hydroxyl groups at P, Br-CDs’ surface. As shown in Fig. 6E, the size varies from 1347.0 to 899.1 nm after the addition of increasing concentration of L-Cys, indicating the collapse of P, Br-CDs/Cu2+ complex. Based on this fluorescence “ON-OFF-ON” phenomenon, a fluorescent sensing scheme for Cu2+ and L-Cys detection based on P, Br-CDs is shown in Fig. 6F.

In order to verify the feasibility of “OFF-ON” sensing method for practical application, the experiments have been designed to detect the samples of Cu2+ and L-Cys (25 µM). The operating steps were conducted in the same conditions and the results were presented in Table 1. The recoveries of Cu2+ were rose from 85.9% to 100.2% with RSD range from 1.0% to 1.5%. While the recoveries of L-Cys were rose from 88.9% to 109.9% with RSD range from 1.8% to 6.4%. The results show that it’s feasible to detect Cu2+ and L-Cys in practical samples.

Novel bright red-emitting P, Br co-doped CDs were synthesized with pPD, DAP and KBr by a hydrothermal method. Compared with pPD-CDs, P, Br co-doped CDs could enhance the emission intensity up to 21 times with 25 nm blue-shift. Furthermore, P, Br-CDs have the remarkable fluorescence turn-off response to Cu2+, and the corresponding mechanism is static quenching. After the addition of L-Cys, the fluorescence could recover visibly. Thus, “OFF-ON” sensing based on P, Br-CDs enable selective detection of Cu2+ and L-Cys with a correlation coefficient of 0.995 and 0.983, in addition, LOD of 4.408 µM and 2.373 µM, respectively. All results suggest that P, Br-CDs have great potential as “OFF-ON” fluorescent probe for Cu2+ and L-cysteine detection.

Weiying Zhou: Writing – original draft preparation. Fuwang Mo: Data curation. Zishan Sun: Investigation. Jiabao Luo: SEM/TEM measurement. Jiaqi Fan: Figure preparation. Hongni Zhu: Temperature-dependent PL measurement. Zhenpeng Zhu: Cell imaging. Jinqing Huang: Writing – review & editing. Xinguo Zhang: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The research was funded by Guangzhou Scientific Planning Program (No. 201804010260), National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21601081), Guangxi Natural Science Foundation (No. 2018GXNSFBA281209) and Open project of College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology (QUSTHX201925). Jinqing Huang acknowledges the financial support from Science, Technology and Innovation Commission of Shenzhen Municipality (No. JCYJ20180306174930894).
